title: Teaching
text:
Universidad Técnica Federico Santa María
Lecturer, Department of Business & Economics · 2019–Present
Santiago and Valparaíso, Chile

Courses taught

Principles of Economics: Fall 2019, Fall 2020, Fall 2021, Fall 2022, Fall 2026
Macroeconomics: Fall 2022, Fall 2024
Microeconomics I: Spring 2026
Microeconomics II: 2019, 2020, 2021, 2022, 2023, 2024
Industrial Organization: Spring 2019

I also serve as a thesis committee co-advisor for undergraduate students.
Teaching recognition: Named Best Professor 2022 by student vote, B.A. in Business & Economics, Campus Santiago-Vitacura.

title: María Pía Santibáñez
text:
I am a 4th year PhD student in Economics at the University of Chile. Before my PhD, I received a M.A. in Economic Policy from Boston University and a B.A. in Business and Economics from Universidad Técnica Federico Santa María.
My research interests are in applied microeconomics, labor economics, development economics, and economic history.
My current research examines the relationship between parental contact with the justice system and adolescent offending in Chile, as part of a broader agenda on children’s life trajectories and intergenerational disadvantage.
Feel free to reach out at marsantiba@fen.uchile.cl.

title: Research
text:
Status: PhD thesis chapter / Working paper
This paper examines the intergenerational transmission of criminal behavior in a developing economy. Using a novel administrative dataset covering the full birth cohort of children born in Chile in 2010, I estimate the association between parental contact with the justice system and adolescent offending through a Linear Probability Model with school and commune fixed effects. I document four dimensions of heterogeneity: offense severity, timing of exposure (strongest at ages 11–14), gender and parental structure, and offense type. Parental incarceration is associated with offending rates approximately 500% above the control mean. The results suggest that intergenerational crime transmission is concentrated among specific high-risk family profiles.
